transcript:
You're already looking at aftermath. That mesh of blue trails, the scattered orange nodes — none of it is starting up when you arrive. It's mid-history, and the piece is built so that's literally true, not just a mood.

Here's the trick: there's a warmup loop, right after the setup, that runs 280 frames of construction *before the first pixel ever hits your screen* — a plain for-loop, no animation, no rendering. Only after that silent history completes does the code render its first visible frame. You never see frame zero. You can't. The comment right above it says it outright: "the viewer arrives into presence, not void."

And notice the colors aren't named by hue — they're named by relationship to time: "already built," "witnessing erases," "ghost of builder." That last one matters — when an expanding decay sphere touches a construction particle, it doesn't destroy it, it just turns it into a ghost, ownership of its own building revoked retroactively.

So the vertigo isn't a metaphor layered on top — it's literally baked into when the render call happens.

cues.context: named by relationship to time: "already built," "witnessing erases," "ghost of builder."
